input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is infrastructure assets within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, such as buildings, characterized by their cyber-physical nature requiring integration of static models with runtime data for simulation, analysis, and optimization. Key characteristics include real-time data integration, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities across the building lifecycle from initial design to refurbishments and operations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including real-time monitoring (via real-time insights), predictive analytics, simulation, analysis, and optimization of infrastructure assets. These services enable data-driven infrastructure management, reduced operational costs, and improved stakeholder collaboration throughout the building lifecycle from design to operation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange enables continuous synchronization between physical and digital components with no specified frequency or latency metrics beyond real-time operation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Centralized orchestration via the DT Framework coordinating roles and components for bidirectional data exchange, enriched BIM model integration, and runtime configuration management to enable holistic building lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability across heterogeneous systems is achieved through standardized BIM-based modeling and modular framework extension points, enabling integration with enterprise systems and external services though specific protocols are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models are based on Building Information Modeling (BIM) geometric representations enriched with Building Control System (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers) defined by Building Control Engineers. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models to automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Real-time data exchange ensures continuous synchronization between physical infrastructure and the digital twin, supported by a unified semantic foundation for model transformation and integration of custom analytics services developed by DT Engineers.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric is used to evaluate the entanglement of the resulting Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, with bidirectional data flow supporting monitoring and control via interactive visualization interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are transmitted from the digital twin to physical building systems through bidirectional communication, enabling Facility Managers to interact with deployed DT for real-time monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange, utilizing standardized protocols to facilitate bidirectional communication between physical assets and digital twins.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework supports the complete DT lifecycle from initial device modeling in BIM (Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators, and controllers), through MDE-based extraction of structured representations and automatic generation of deployable artifacts, to runtime operations with real-time data exchange and interactive visualization for Facility Managers, and evolution via modular integration of custom services by DT Engineers.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles are used to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. The modular architecture provides standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, enabling DT evolution in response to changing operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control of physical infrastructure. Analytics capabilities are integrated through custom services within the modular architecture, supporting Facility Manager operations.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning:
The extraction accurately reflects the text's definition of the system as infrastructure assets in the AECO domain. It correctly identifies the cyber-physical nature, the integration of static models with runtime data, and the lifecycle management capabilities.

Supporting quotes: 'Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building)'; 'DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'; 'improved stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation'.

The description correctly lists the specific services provided by the Digital Twin, such as real-time monitoring and simulation, as well as the resulting benefits like reduced operational costs.

Supporting quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization of building operations'; 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'; 'promises tangible benefits such as reduced operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved stakeholder collaboration'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly mentions specific tools and frameworks used for implementation, such as Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine, and Building Information Modeling (BIM). Supported by quotes: 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', and 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description correctly states that the system enables real-time data exchange and that specific latency metrics are not provided. The source confirms 'real-time data exchange' in the abstract and 'live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins' in the R3 contribution, but does not provide specific frequencies or granularities.

The provided text focuses on a framework for deploying Digital Twins for buildings and a specific 'Living Lab case study.' While it mentions 'scalable DT deployment' and 'service-oriented DT platforms,' it does not describe the coordination mechanisms between multiple digital twin instances, a hierarchy of twins, or inter-twin multiplicities. Therefore, the assessment that this is 'Not in Document' is correct.

The description accurately summarizes the orchestration, mentioning the 'centralized' role of the DT framework, 'bidirectional data exchange', and 'BIM model integration'. The source states 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange' and discusses the holistic approach to building life cycle management.

The description correctly identifies that interoperability is based on 'standardized BIM-based modeling' and 'modular framework extension points', and notes that protocols are not detailed. The source confirms the framework is 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)' and has 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

Therefore, the extraction correctly identifies this as 'Not in Document'.

While the document mentions using Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and discusses general system architecture, it does not provide details on cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, fail-safe mechanisms, or risk assessment approaches. Consequently, the extraction correctly states this is 'Not in Document'.
